title: MW‐LEACH: Low energy adaptive clustering hierarchy approach for WSN

sections.name: Body
sections.content:
IET Wireless Sensor Systems
Research Article
MW-LEACH: Low energy adaptive clustering
hierarchy approach for WSN
ISSN 2043-6386
Received on 23rd October 2019
Revised 7th January 2020
Accepted on 15th January 2020
E-First on 3rd April 2020
doi: 10.1049/iet-wss.2019.0195
www.ietdl.org
Salim El Khediri1,2
 , Rehan Ullah Khan1,2, Nejah Nasri3, Abdennaceur Kachouri3
1Information Technology Department, College of Computer, Qassim University, Buraidah, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
2Intelligent Analytics Group (IAG), Information Technology Department, College of Computer, Qassim University, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
3National Engineering School of Sfax, la Soukra, Sfax, 3038 Tunisia
 E-mail: s.elkhediri@qu.edu.sa
Abstract: The authors propose a novel multiple weight low energy adaptive clustering hierarchy (MW-LEACH) protocol for
wireless sensor networks. In MW-LEACH, they select the cluster heads (CHs) based on the residual energy, the distances
between the CHs, and an optimal number of member nodes. The nodes are selected from the initial set based on the high
residual energy closer to the centre of the density, thus forming an initial set of CH candidates. The candidates then move in
different directions to collect data from their members sending it to the base station. The proposed approach has lower
complexity in terms of time and message. It is also fast and offers a longer lifetime for the network. It also provides a proper
level of fault tolerance. In the experimental simulation evaluation, their approach outperforms state-of-the-art protocols based on
performance metrics of throughput, energy consumption, packet delivery, network lifetime, and latency.

sections.name: Introduction
sections.content:
Recently, there has been rapid progress in wireless sensor networks
(WSNs), because of their applications in many areas such as
monitoring environment, security surveillance, seismic control, and
other similar areas. WSN comprises many sensor nodes that are
connected wirelessly. Each sensor in the WSN plays the role of
capturing the corresponding events and routing them. The sensor
node management system is used to monitor specific goals and
collect information. The data is then transmitted by these nodes to
the sink or the base station (BS) wirelessly [ 1, 2]. The BS is then
further connected to a switching centre, which is a gateway to the
internet. Algorithm 1 (see Fig. 1) depicts protocol in the WSNs,
where the sensor node sends the information directly to the BS.
The key point in any WSN model is extending the lifetime of
WSN nodes. The batteries of the nodes cannot be recharged or
replaced in most of the cases once they are exhausted [ 3].
Traditional deployment of nodes in WSN is not efficient due to the
complexity and power demands [ 4]. Many constraints and several
requirements bound the synchronisation process. The optimal CH
selection is a challenge for all clustering algorithms, which, in
essence, are the multiple criteria for decision making [ 5, 6]. In this
study, we propose a novel method for selecting CH. Our approach
is based on the amount of energy left after each repetitive cycle. As
such, the minimum energy for the selected CH is estimated as a
prior. The proposed hierarchical protocol uses the ‘threshold’
energy value that is used to prevent group leader creation. This
approach thus ensures reliable performance of the WSN.
2 Multiple weight low energy adaptive clustering
hierarchy (MW-LEACH) protocol
The steps of the proposed MW-LEACH protocol are shown in
Algorithm 1 (see Fig. 1). MW-LEACH employs the uniform
clustering approach for minimising energy consumption and
prolonging the network life. It achieves the reduction in energy
consumption by reducing the distance for the data transmission in
WSN with the help of the uniform cluster structure, and multi-hop
approaches. For this, the proposed MW-LEACH adopts the
centralised and cluster-based approaches for cluster routing
structure for the nodes. The distance among the nodes is calculated,
and for selecting the CH nodes, the remaining energy of sensor
nodes is taken into account. Also, for each CH, the number of
nodes is calculated. With the uniform cluster distribution and
locations, the distances for data transmission between the nodes are
optimally reduced by the adaptive multi-hop approach. As such,
the MW-LEACH tackles not only the fluctuations of the network
conditions but also accounts for variable environments in practical
circumstances. In the simulation setup, different nodes and sensing
locations are considered and analysed. The proposed MW-LEACH
thus reduces the consumption of the energy in the WSN and
increases its lifetime by load balancing in the clusters. In the
proposed MW-LEACH, the sensor nodes can also become CH for
gathering data, compresses it and sends it to the BS. The constraint
for all the nodes is that they are homogeneous and have limited
energy.
3 Algorithmic parameters
WSN consumes energy and several factors can influence this
energy consumption, e.g. mobility and repetitive transmission.
Fig. 1  Algorithm 1: CHs selection phase

IET Wirel. Sens. Syst., 2020, Vol. 10 Iss. 3, pp. 126-129
© The Institution of Engineering and Technology 2020
126

Besides, the weight of the considered nodes during CH creation,
the network topology also affects energy usage. If the distance is
significant, the one-hop transmission quickly depletes the batteries.
The k-jumps are a useful approach in this case; however, it
weakens the consecutive node's energy. In the following, we
analyse the weight effect on energy consumption. For the energy
minimisation in the proposed model, we analyse the impact of
varying weights and determine the optimal weight energy. The
weights of nodes in the cluster heads (CHs) selection phase are
calculated as
Weight i = Ec i + 1
D i + n × Dis i (1)
The value of n affects weights. We tested three weights with n
values: n = 0, 1, 1/200, and evaluated the properties of CH
selection.
For the first case of weight, when n = 0, nodes weight is
Weight i = Ec i + 1
D i (2)
Weight selects the nodes having maximum residual energy with a
maximum degree that is independent of the distance to the BS.
For the second weight calculation, n = 1, we get
Weight i = Ec i + 1
D i + Dis i (3)
The nodes residual energy is 0.5j and
0 ≤ Ec i ≤ 0.5 J (4)
As the number of nodes is N, therefore
0 ≤ 1
D i ≤ 1
N − 1 ≤ 1 (5)
A node being 10 m from the BS. The BS maximum distance is 200,
so we have
10 ≤ DisBS i ≤ 200 (6)
Thus, energy and degree are negligible with the distance to the BS
For the third weight, n = 1/200, we get
Weight i = Ec i + 1
D i + DisBS
200 (7)
Equation (7) shows that the energy is at maximum, and the degree
is also maximum being the closest to the BS. Fig. 2 presents a
justification of the weight selection. It can be seen that the third
weight gets lower energy consumption. Therefore, the energy,
level, and distance are the variables that CHs have to take into
account.
4 Algorithm for CH's election
The pseudo algorithm for the CH selection phase is shown in
Fig. 1.
From Algorithm 1 (Fig. 1), the CH with energy is selected. The
selected CH node relays its CH status to other nodes by
‘HEAD_Adv_Msg’ message. A ‘wait’ procedure starts for the
addition of member nodes into clusters. For cluster arrangements,
the CH creates its Time-Division Multiple Access [TDMA] table
and relays it to the member nodes. For transmission to the BS, the
CH uses the code division multiple access technique.
5 Simulation results and discussion
For simulation and comparative analysis, the total nodes are
assumed to be 10–300. The nodes are homogeneous, randomly
selected, and distributed in a 100 m 2 area. The BS is fixed in the
centre of the sensing area. Also, we do not take into account the
signal collision and interference. The nodes have an initial energy
of 0.5 J. The network is a clustered hierarchy, where CH fuses data
to reduce the redundancy and correlation. It is also assumed that
we have static nodes. This avoids the change of topology. Table 1
shows the simulation parameters.
For performance evaluation, the proposed MW-LEACH
approach is compared with the BeeCluster [ 7], O-LEACH [8], and
LEACH [ 4] protocols. The comparison is based on the three
primary factors:
i. The number of alive nodes.
ii. Energy consumption.
iii. Packet delivery ratio.
Figs. 3 and 4 show the comparison of the MW-LEACH with
BeeCluster, O-LEACH, and LEACH for the lifetime of the
network. Fig. 3 shows that as the number of rounds increases, the
number of live nodes in BeeCluster, O-LEACH, and LEACH
keeps decreasing. For example, in round 2000, out of 100 nodes,
all the nodes are still alive in the case of MW-LEACH, while for
the BeeCluster, there are 90 live nodes. The number of active
Fig. 2  Impact of weight on CH selection. ‘Weight 1’ is for n  = 0, ‘weight
2’ is for n  = 1, and ‘weight 3’ is for n  = 1/200. Y-axis is the energy
consumption

Fig. 3   MW-LEACH comparison for 100 sensor nodes. Y-axis is the
number of alive nodes

nodes for the O-LEACH is 70, and for the LEACH, the live nodes
are 57. A similar trend can be seen for the rounds 2500, 3500, and
4000 in Fig. 3. For the round 4500, the proposed MW-LEACH,
BeeCluster, and O-LEACH have live nodes. However, all the
nodes of the LEACH are exhausted. The live nodes for round 5000
only exist for the proposed MW-LEACH. All three comparative
approaches have exhausted their active nodes in round 5000. The
MW-LEACH has seven live nodes at that instance.
Fig. 4 shows the number of live nodes versus the number of
rounds for 300 rounds. Like that of Fig. 3, a similar trend can be
seen in Fig. 4 for 300 nodes. For round 500, the live nodes of
LEACH are reduced to 185. For round 2000, the live nodes in
MW-LEACH are still 300, while for the BeeCluster, O-LEACH,
and LEACH, the live nodes are 260, 225, and 110, respectively.
The LEACH has already lost more than 50% of the live nodes. In
Fig. 4, the trend of the live nodes keeps decreasing for the increase
in rounds. For round 4500, the MW-LEACH has 65 live nodes,
while the BeeCluster and O-LEACH have 25 and 11 active nodes,
respectively.
From Figs. 3 and 4, in all cases, the proposed MW-LEACH gets
more live nodes compared to BeeCluster, O-LEACH, and LEACH.
This is due to the availability of not only surplus energy to the
nodes but also due to the optimal selection of CHs with the
proposed approach. The availability of surplus energy thus is
achieved due to the reduced number of computations in the entire
network.
Fig. 5 shows the comparative analysis of the proposed MWLEACH using the energy consumption factor. In Fig. 5, when the
number of nodes is 10, the energy of the nodes for the MWLEACH is (more than) 50% higher than the BeeCluster, OLEACH, and LEACH. This trend is also seen with the increasing
number of nodes. For the number of nodes equal to 150, the
proposed MW-LEACH has 27% higher energy than the
BeeCluster, 30% compared to the O-LEACH, and 35% compared
to the LEACH approach. With the number of nodes equal to 200,
the energy of the MW-LEACH still outperforms other approaches.
Fig. 6 shows the comparative analysis of the proposed MWLEACH based on the packet delivery ratio with the BeeCluster, OLEACH, and LEACH. In Fig. 6, when the number of nodes is 50,
and when the MW-LEACH sends the packets, there was no error in
the information. This means that the MW-LEACH gets 100%
accurate information. However, for the BeeCluster, O-LEACH, and
LEACH, accuracy is found to be 90, 86, and 82%, respectively.
The trend is repeated for the increasing number of nodes. For 300
nodes, the MW-LEACH loses 20% information. The BeeCluster
loses 22%, O-LEACH loses 24%, and LEACH loses 30% of the
information.
From Figs. 3–6, it can be seen that based on the three
comparative factors, the proposed MW-LEACH approach shows
better performance in all cases. For the number of live nodes, the
MW-LEACH outperforms all other approaches by a considerable
margin. For energy consumption scenarios, the MW-LEACH
provides better energy conservation. For the packet delivery ratio,
the MW-LEACH provides accurate information representation
compared to the BeeCluster, O-LEACH, and LEACH approaches.
These results confirm the efficacy of the MW-LEACH algorithm
compared to the other algorithms. As the proposed approach is
based on multiple parameters for CH selection, the energy is
conserved in the network. The lifetime of a network is generally
based on the battery capacity of nodes. The proposed approach of
altering the CH based on the residual energy and other parameters
improves battery life, connectivity, and thus increases the overall
lifetime of the WSN. We can deduce that the absence of a clear
data aggregation and communication infrastructure especially for a
big network in the BeeCluster, O-LEACH, and LEACH protocols
results in reduced network lifetime.

sections.name: Conclusion
sections.content:
We proposed a novel clustering algorithm in the form of the MWLEACH. As such the CHs are selected based on residual energy
and the distances between the CHs. The nodes are selected from
the initial set based on the high residual energy closer to the centre
of the density, thus forming an initial set of CH candidates. In the
experimental evaluation, our approach outperforms state-of-the-art
BeeCluster, O-LEACH, and LEACH protocols based on
performance metrics of throughput, energy consumption, packet
delivery, network lifetime, and latency. In the future, we want to
implement the network scenarios on a real testbed of sensors with a
specific application domain.
